content.full_text:
Employer Reimburses Employee For Personal Expenses - Contribution

Regulation 18423 provides that salary, reimbursement for personal expenses and other compensation provided by an employer to his employee who spends less than 10% of his compensated time on political activities is not a contribution. Thus, if an employer pays the salary and expenses of an employee to fly from Sacramento to Los Angeles to work on a campaign for a day and one-half, the employer will not have made a contribution to the campaign. This is because a day and one-half is less than 10% of the employee's compensated time and therefore the regulation specifically states that the salary and personal expenses such as transportation, accommodations and meals are not contributions.

This regulation is not applicable to an employer who provides reimbursement for personal expenses to an employee who is not on compensated time. Thus, if an employee takes vacation time and the employer pays his personal expenses while he engages in political activities, all of the personal expenses are reportable contributions.
